mnemonics.title: SPUD for Basic Operators
mnemonics.content: Selection, Projection, Union, Difference - remember as 'SPUD' - these are the fundamental operators in Relational Algebra that form the basis for all other operations.

mnemonics.title: Sigma Selects Rows
mnemonics.content: The selection operator (σ) selects rows/tuples that satisfy a condition - think 'Sigma Sifts' through data to find matching rows.

mnemonics.title: Pi Picks Columns
mnemonics.content: The projection operator (π) picks specific columns and eliminates duplicates - remember 'Pi Picks' only the columns you need.

mnemonics.title: Rho Renames Relations
mnemonics.content: The rename operator (ρ) is represented by rho (ρ) - think 'Rho Renames' to change relation or attribute names.

mnemonics.title: Join = Product + Select
mnemonics.content: Natural Join (⋈) combines tables by matching common attributes - think of it as 'Cartesian Product + Selection on common attributes'.

mnemonics.title: Division is Inverse of Product
mnemonics.content: Division (÷) finds attributes that relate to all values in another relation - remember 'A ÷ B gives attributes from A that match every B'.

acronyms.usage: Used to filter rows based on a condition. Example: σ_{salary>50000}(Employee) selects employees earning more than 50000.
Used to select specific columns/attributes. Example: π_{name,salary}(Employee) shows only name and salary columns.
Used to rename relations or attributes for clarity or to handle self-joins. Example: ρ_{newName}(oldRelation).
Combines results of two relations, removing duplicates. Both relations must have compatible schemas (same number and domain of attributes).
Returns tuples present in both relations. Equivalent to R ∩ S = R - (R - S). Both must have compatible schemas.
Returns tuples in first relation but not in second. Also requires compatible schemas. R - S gives tuples in R not in S.
Combines all tuples from two relations. If R has n tuples and S has m tuples, R×S has n×m tuples.
Returns attributes from first relation that match ALL values in second relation. Used for 'for all' type queries.
Joins relations on common attributes (with same name), automatically eliminating duplicate common column.
Joins based on a specific condition (θ). R ⋈_θ S = σ_θ(R × S). Also called θ-join.
Outer joins preserve tuples from one or both relations that don't have matching values, filling with NULLs.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a horizontal filter or sieve - only rows that pass through the condition are selected. Think of it like a food strainer letting only desired items through.
Picture a vertical slice or column extraction - you're 'slicing' down to show only specific columns. Imagine a spreadsheet where you hide certain columns.
Think of a multiplication table - every row from table A combines with every row from table B. Visualize as all possible pairings.
Picture two tables being stitched together along matching columns (like names or IDs). Like connecting puzzle pieces where the shapes match.
Think of two circles in a Venn diagram - union is the entire shaded area including overlap. But remember it's like stacking two lists and removing duplicates.
Visualize as subtracting one circle from another - what's left in the first but not in the second. Like removing common elements.
Think of it as 'for each A, does it have all B?'. Picture checking if every item in divisor is matched with every item in dividend.

analogies.analogy: Like applying filters on an e-commerce website - you select products with price > 5000, brand = Samsung, etc. The filter condition determines which rows pass through.
Like looking at only specific columns in an Excel sheet - if you only want to see Name and Phone columns, you 'project' to those columns and hide others.
Like generating all possible combinations in a restaurant menu - if you have 3 appetizers and 4 main courses, the product gives 3×4=12 possible meal combinations.
Like matching students to their admitted courses in a college - you join on StudentID which exists in both the Students table and Admissions table.
Like finding pairs of friends where one is older than the other - the condition (age comparison) specifies exactly which combinations qualify.
Like combining two lists of cities from different travel guides and removing duplicates - you get all unique cities from both lists.
Like finding which students have attended ALL scheduled classes - if you have 'Attendance(Student, Class)' and 'AllClasses(Class)', Division gives students who match every class.
